subject: ap-psychology
theme: Cognition encompasses how humans encode and store memories, solve problems and make decisions, use language, and perceive the sensory world — all shaped by attention, heuristics, and the limits of conscious awareness.

core_concepts.title: Memory Encoding & Storage Models
core_concepts.detail: Memory encoding follows levels of processing: shallow (structural/phonemic) encoding produces weaker memory traces; deep semantic encoding produces the most durable memories. The Atkinson-Shiffrin model describes three stores: sensory memory (iconic ≈ 0.5 sec, echoic ≈ 3-4 sec) → short-term memory (≈7±2 items, ≈20 sec) → long-term memory (unlimited capacity and duration).

core_concepts.title: Working Memory & Long-Term Potentiation
core_concepts.detail: Working memory (Baddeley) is the active workspace that holds and manipulates information through the phonological loop (verbal), visuospatial sketchpad (visual/spatial), and central executive (attention controller). Long-term potentiation (LTP) — sustained synaptic strengthening — is the cellular basis of long-term memory formation.

core_concepts.title: Reconstructive Memory
core_concepts.detail: Memory is reconstructive, not reproductive. The misinformation effect (Loftus) shows that post-event information distorts memory. Context-dependent and state-dependent memory demonstrate that retrieval is improved when encoding and retrieval contexts match.

core_concepts.title: Perception & Depth Cues
core_concepts.detail: Perception involves transduction (sensory-to-neural conversion), bottom-up processing (data-driven), and top-down processing (expectation/context-driven). Key depth cues: binocular — retinal disparity, convergence; monocular — linear perspective, relative size, texture gradient, interposition. Gestalt principles (proximity, similarity, closure, figure-ground) organize perception automatically.

core_concepts.title: Problem Solving & Decision Heuristics
core_concepts.detail: Problem solving is impaired by functional fixedness (seeing objects only in their typical uses) and mental set (persisting with a prior approach). Decision making relies on heuristics: the availability heuristic (judging probability by ease of recall) and representativeness heuristic (judging by similarity to a prototype) both produce systematic biases.

core_concepts.title: Language Development
core_concepts.detail: Language development follows a universal sequence: cooing → babbling (all phonemes) → perceptual narrowing to native phonemes → one-word (holophrase) → two-word → telegraphic speech. Chomsky's nativist theory posits an innate Language Acquisition Device; Vygotsky emphasized the social context of language development.

core_concepts.title: Selective Attention & Serial Position
core_concepts.detail: Selective attention filters perception — inattentional blindness (missing obvious events during focused attention) and the cocktail party effect (noticing one's own name in background noise) illustrate how attention governs conscious experience. The serial position effect shows superior recall for primacy (rehearsal) and recency (working memory) items.

exam_tip: FRQ prompts may require distinguishing specific memory systems (working vs. long-term), naming the correct heuristic, or identifying which Gestalt principle or depth cue explains a scenario — precision with terminology is required for full credit.
